participants.reasoning: The extracted participants description matches the official baseline characteristics, inclusion/exclusion criteria, recruitment setting, and randomized sample size with only minor wording differences.
participants.extracted: Employees recruited from six Dutch companies (two banking companies, two research institutes, one security company, and one university) who expressed interest in the study. Eligible participants were adults with elevated depressive symptoms (CES-D score ≥16) who were not on sick leave at baseline, had internet and email access, and were not excluded for recent antidepressant use (<1 month) or a legal labor dispute. A total of 231 employees were randomized; the mean age was 43.4 years, 62.3% were female, 95.2% were born in the Netherlands, 76.2% were in a relationship, and 63.6% were highly educated. Most participants worked white-collar jobs and averaged 34 hours per week.

interventions.reasoning: The extracted description matches the official intervention details on content, structure, duration, tunneling, coaching, fidelity review, and treatment-completer criteria.
interventions.extracted: Happy@Work was a brief, web-based guided self-help intervention with minimal coaching. It combined problem-solving treatment, cognitive therapy, and a guideline for employees aimed at preventing work-related stress. The program consisted of 6 weekly lessons, with an option of 1 extra week if delayed. Each lesson included information, examples, and assignments, covering introduction to problem solving, problem-solving methods, changing cognitions, dealing with work-related problems, social support, and relapse prevention. Participants received feedback on assignments from trained master’s-level clinical psychology students, and all feedback was reviewed by a supervisor to ensure fidelity. The intervention was tunneled, meaning participants could only start the next lesson after receiving feedback. Participants were considered treatment completers if they completed at least the basic information and assignments from the first 3 lessons.
interventions.official:
Intervention characteristics
Psychological intervention: I‐CBT plus PST, I‐guided
Content: Happy@Work: a brief Web‐based intervention delivered with minimal guidance; consists of 2 evidence‐based treatments: problem‐solving treatment (PST) and cognitive therapy (CT) [and a guideline for employees to help them to prevent work‐related stress; Web‐based lessons has a different theme, but always follows the same structure: information about the theme, examples, and assignments. outcomes.reasoning: The extracted outcomes include multiple planned measures and timepoints, while the official outcomes list only sickness absence at 6 months and depressive symptoms (CES-D), with mismatched wording and incomplete outcome/timepoint coverage.
outcomes.extracted: The trial planned to measure depressive symptoms as the primary outcome using the Center for Epidemiologic Studies Depression scale (CES-D; 20 items, total score 0-60) at baseline, posttreatment at 8 weeks, 6 months, and 12 months. Secondary outcomes included burnout symptoms using the Dutch version of the Maslach Burnout Inventory-General Scale (MBI; 15 items across emotional exhaustion, cynicism, and reduced professional efficacy dimensions, scored 0-6 per item and summarized by dimension) at baseline, 8 weeks, 6 months, and 12 months; work absenteeism using the second part of the Trimbos and iMTA Questionnaire on Costs Associated with Psychiatric Illness / Short Form Health and Labor Questionnaire (TiC-P / SF-HLQ) assessing days absent from work due to illness over the interval between assessments, with baseline recall covering the prior 3 months and follow-up intervals of baseline to 8 weeks, 8 weeks to 6 months, and 6 to 12 months; work performance using item 4 of the World Health Organization Health and Work Performance Questionnaire (WHO-HPQ; 7-point Likert rating of overall work performance compared with comparable employees) at baseline, 8 weeks, 6 months, and 12 months; and anxiety symptoms using the anxiety subscale of the Hospital Anxiety and Depression Scale (HADS-A; 7 items, total score 0-21) at baseline, 8 weeks, 6 months, and 12 months. A structured diagnostic interview, the WHO Composite International Diagnostic Interview (CIDI 2.1), was also administered at baseline and 6 months for mood and anxiety disorders.
